tweets.text:
China's rare-earth exports to the US remain critically low:

China's monthly exports of rare-earth magnets to the US averaged 479 tons in H1 2026, on track for the lowest annual monthly average since 2021.

By comparison, exports averaged 586 tons between 2022 and 2024, ~22% higher than the current rate.

These components are critical to industries spanning electric vehicles, industrial automation, and defense, making secure access to them a key priority for US manufacturers.

This comes despite October's truce between President Trump and President Xi, in which China pledged to keep rare-earth magnets flowing as a key part of pausing the trade war.

China remains the dominant force in the industry, controlling over 90% of global rare-earth magnet production.

Key shipments of rare-earth magnets from China are constrained.

tweets.text:
A smaller piece of every US Dollar earned in the economy is going to workers:

The proportion of wages and salaries in US gross domestic income (GDI) is down to ~43%, near the lowest since records began in 1929.

Gross domestic income measures the total income earned across the economy, including wages, corporate profits, and investment income.

This metric has been in a long-term decline since peaking at ~52% in the 1940s.

By comparison, between the 1940s and the 1960s, this percentage never fell below 48%.

In other words, a larger proportion of economic income is now flowing to corporate profits and investment income, rather than pay workers.

The gap between corporate profits and labor income keeps widening.

tweets.text:
China is leading the global nuclear race:

China now has 39 nuclear reactors under construction, more than the next 12 countries combined.

This is followed by India and Russia, at 8 and 7, respectively, less than half of China's total combined.

China also has a much younger pipeline of nuclear projects, with reactors currently under construction averaging just ~2.8 years since work began.

By comparison, the global average stands at ~5.8 years, inflated by long-delayed projects, such as India's 22-year construction timeline and Brazil's 16-year timeline.

Meanwhile, the US currently has no large commercial nuclear reactors under construction.

China is building the world's largest pipeline of nuclear reactors.

tweets.text:
Applied Intuition Co-founder @qasar on why timing decides who wins in the new technology market:

 "The key thing in the new technology business ... It's when and how you deploy them into the market. The 'when' becomes really important."

"You're two years early and you're doomed. You're two years late, there's too many competitors. You have to hit at the right spot."

"The market is ready to consume, and I think you just have to meet the market in the way that, the best way possible."

@AppliedInt

tweets.text:
Bearish bets on US stocks are surging:

Short interest in the S&P 500 is up to ~3.7% of its free float, near the highest in data going back to 2010.

Short interest in the Russell 3000 is up to ~6.1%, also near an all-time high.

Both metrics have steadily increased since the start of 2025.

Furthermore, short interest across all NYSE-listed stocks rose to a record 9.0% of shares outstanding in late June.

By comparison, this metric peaked at ~5.0% during the 2008 Financial Crisis and ~6.0% during the 2020 pandemic.

Conditions for a short-squeeze are rising.

tweets.text:
The cost of labor in the US has never been higher.

US average hourly earnings hit $37.64 in June, a record high and up +3.5% over the last year.

Even for online stores, a single customer service hire now runs around $4,000 per month.

As a result, the global AI customer service market is rapidly expanding.

Between 2024 and 20233, the global AI customer service market is expected to grow from $13 billion to $84 billion.

This implies a +23.2% annual growth rate with expectations of a -5% decline in customer service employment over the next decade.

As labor costs continue rising, the return on investment for AI has become increasingly compelling.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
We’ve raised over $100M to create AI customer support that doesn’t sound like AI slop.

Introducing Gorgias AI Agent 3.0: the first AI customer support that’s better than any human.

How it works 👇 https://t.co/GqCJNc7Xiz

tweets.text:
.@AppliedInt Co-founders @qasar and Peter Ludwig on why tools like Dana will make building autonomous robots as easy as building iPhone apps:

"Why can't a ninth grader make a delivery robot in their home?"

"The robot runs into the wall. Okay, what happened there? The loop closes."

"That platform for designing and developing is what we're launching, it's called Dana."

"If you look at how tooling has changed in the digital AI world... now everything has moved into a very different, almost IDE. We think the same thing's going to happen in the physical world."

"Fundamentally, Dana is our agentic platform for physical AI. And everything that we've built and developed over the past nearly a decade, every tool, every technique, that's available in Dana."

"And it's very easy to use with the agentic interface. And so workflows that used to maybe take days or weeks to run, you can now run those in minutes in many cases."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
For 9+ years we’ve been building simulators, data engines, data pipelines, scenario editors, RL environments, what's now world models... and dozens of other products.

Now we’ve connected them all under one agentic layer.

It is not just another harness but also the broader https://t.co/Fk3U3feQ5i
tweets.full_text:
.@AppliedInt Co-founders @qasar and Peter Ludwig on why tools like Dana will make building autonomous robots as easy as building iPhone apps:

"Why can't a ninth grader make a delivery robot in their home?"

"That platform for designing and developing is what we're launching, it's called Dana."

"If you look at how tooling has changed in the digital AI world... now everything has moved into a very different, almost IDE. We think the same thing's going to happen in the physical world."

"Fundamentally, Dana is our agentic platform for physical AI. And everything that we've built and developed over the past nearly a decade, every tool, every technique, that's available in Dana."

"And it's very easy to use with the agentic interface. And so workflows that used to maybe take days or weeks to run, you can now run those in minutes in many cases."

tweets.text:
The AI boom is transforming one of the biggest deflationary forces into a key source of US inflation:

Import prices for computer and electronic products surged +7.4% in the first 6 months of 2026, to 77.1 points, the highest since January 2016.

This also marks a +8.0% YoY jump, the largest annual increase on record.

This represents a historic reversal from a downtrend which persisted for over two decades.

Between 2006 and 2019, computer and electronic product import prices fell -30%, as improving technology and manufacturing efficiency consistently pushed costs lower.

Now, the AI investment boom is straining manufacturing capacity for semiconductors, servers, and electronic components, as companies race to build out AI infrastructure regardless of cost.

The AI revolution has flipped technology from a long-term source of deflation into a major driver of inflation.

tweets.text:
This stat for people most likely to be on the left of the liberal spectrum is the least surprising thing i've ever seen. It honestly makes completely sense to me from multiple personal anecdotes. Here's one:

One of the most liberal people I've ever met fell into this camp. She lived in a nice apartment in Brooklyn. At least one Masters degree but maybe more. Worked for a non-profit in NYC helping underprivileged people with housing and other things (obviously a good & kind thing). But *constantly* talked about politics. Railed against capitalism in some way every time i saw her -- either overtly or with side comments on things. Was honestly just too much. I'd bet with near certainty she'd describe herself as a dem socialist today.

Anyway... I eventually learned she afforded the nice Brooklyn apartment because her father paid her rent and just generally financially supported her lifestyle in NYC

The best part is that her father was a successful former wall street executive that retired early...😂

Haven't spoke to or seen her since covid lockdowns though. She was mid 30s and single at the time. She has to be nearing 40 now. No idea of her current situation. Almost like a caricature of a person looking back on it now.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: In today's newsletter, I argue this is the sweet spot for the DSA. DSA members are *very* college-educated (80% BA+) but *not* particularly well-off (45% HHI incomes below $60K). Their class status is ambiguous. But they tend to be very liberal on culture as well as economics. https://t.co/yXAW5pVczi

One member watched memory stocks go parabolic, looked at his portfolio, and thought "what if I just... buy them all."

He did it with one ticker: $DRAM. Up 9.13%

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 Chinese Banks, Insurers Increase A-Share Investment - China Securities Journal

➡ Chinese banks and insurers are stepping up equity investment and dividend payout, reflecting confidence in the long-term outlook for the economy and capital markets, China Securities Journal says in a report.

➡ China Life Asset Management bought more than 10 billion yuan ($1.5 billion) worth of equities and equity funds in a single day recently.

➡ Industrial Bank’s subsidiaries in wealth management, trust and funds have significantly expanded products involving equities.

➡ Banks and insurers are also paying out higher dividends to strengthen long-term investor confidence
In a separate report, the paper says that stock ETFs have attracted more than 385 billion yuan in net inflows so far this month.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/ov01fsp5M6

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 #China Seen Stepping Up Fiscal Spending in 2H 2026 - Securities Times

*China is expected to accelerate fiscal spending in the second half as substantial budgeted funds and government bond quotas remain unused, Securities Times says in a report.

*Government bond sales were slow in 1H, leaving 2.3 trillion yuan ($340 billion) worth of local government special bonds to be issued in 2H as well as 728 billion yuan worth of special sovereign bonds.

*Economists expect Beijing’s planned 800 billion yuan new policy financing tool to launch in the third quarter to boost major construction projects.

*The government still has additional funds to deploy without raising the budget deficit. It can use money left over from previous years or sell idling land.

*Fiscal policy is likely to take a bigger role in supporting growth in the second half, as monetary policy is constrained by things including the US-China yield gap and banks’ net interest rate margin.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/ZwoW1cXMD8
tweets.full_text:
🇨🇳 #China Seen Stepping Up Fiscal Spending in 2H 2026 - Securities Times

*China is expected to accelerate fiscal spending in the second half as substantial budgeted funds and government bond quotas remain unused, Securities Times says in a report.

*Government bond sales were slow in 1H, leaving 2.3 trillion yuan ($340 billion) worth of local government special bonds to be issued in 2H as well as 728 billion yuan worth of special sovereign bonds.

*Economists expect Beijing’s planned 800 billion yuan new policy financing tool to launch in the third quarter to boost major construction projects.

*The government still has additional funds to deploy without raising the budget deficit. It can use money left over from previous years or sell idling land.

*Fiscal policy is likely to take a bigger role in supporting growth in the second half, as monetary policy is constrained by things including the US-China yield gap and banks’ net interest rate margin.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/ZwoW1cXMD8

tweets.text:
“The United States is unique because we have this wonderful test bed of entrepreneurial talent. Everyone wants to come here. They want to build.”

But regulatory uncertainty is putting that advantage at risk. @Collin_McCune on why America needs durable crypto legislation:

“We all know people that are going and building in other places because they can’t deal with the wishy-washy or the gray area of the United States.”

“Regulators can step in and use their existing authorities to write some rules. But nothing can replace long-term legislation.”

@milkroaddaily

tweets.text:
Transfer activity of tokenized stocks is on the rise. Monthly transfer volume for tokenized stocks reached $9.22 billion in June, up from $53 million last June — a more than 170x increase. This metric measures any onchain movements, such as trading, transfers between wallets, or collateral deposits into DeFi protocols.

The market for tokenized stocks is still a small one compared to traditional equities, which trade in the double-digit trillions per month. But the trend is clear: More issuers and platforms are bringing tokenized stocks online, and the category is growing.

The market for tokenized stocks is still a small one compared to traditional equities, which trade in the double-digit trillions per month. But the trend is clear: More issuers and platforms are bringing tokenized stocks online, and the category is growing.
